title: Galatians 3
subtitle: The Law, the Promise, and Faith

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — "Did You Receive the Spirit by the Law or by Believing?"

heb.paragraph: Paul’s argument from experience: the Galatians received the Spirit not through Torah observance but through the hearing of faith (ex akoēs pisteōs, v. 2). The contrast between Spirit and flesh in v. 3 is salvation-historical: ‘flesh’ refers to human effort (especially circumcision), while ‘Spirit’ represents the eschatological gift marking the new covenant era.

In v. 8 Paul asserts that Scripture ‘announced the gospel in advance to Abraham.’ The verb is a hapax legomenon in the NT. By identifying God’s promise to Abraham as a pre-proclamation of the gospel, Paul roots the Gentile mission in the Abrahamic covenant.

refs.note: The foundational text for justification by faith. Abraham believed God’s promise, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness—prior to circumcision and prior to the Mosaic law by centuries.
God’s promise—‘all peoples on earth will be blessed through you’—is identified in Gal 3:8 as the advance proclamation of the gospel.

notes.note: The appeal to experience is direct: the Galatians received the Spirit by hearing with faith. If the Spirit came through faith, then adding law-works is regression, not progress.
Abraham is the test case. He was justified by faith four centuries before the law was given. If Abraham was justified apart from the law, the law cannot be the basis of justification for anyone.

Calvin explains that ‘foolish’ does not mean intellectually deficient but spiritually dull. The ‘bewitching’ metaphor suggests that false doctrine operates like a spell, clouding the mind to obvious truth.
Calvin finds in Abraham the permanent paradigm of justification by faith. The promise to Abraham is the gospel itself, preached in advance. The Mosaic law did not revoke this prior covenant of grace.
The phrase ex akoēs pisteōs is debated. The NET translators favour ‘by hearing with faith,’ emphasising the human response of believing in the proclaimed message.
The personification of Scripture treats the written text as God’s own voice. The compound verb proeuēngelisato is found only here in the NT.

Moo identifies two parallel arguments: experiential (vv. 1–5) and scriptural (vv. 6–9), both reaching the same conclusion. The Abraham argument is compressed here compared to Romans 4, reflecting the letter’s urgency.

Bruce draws attention to the visual imagery: Christ was ‘publicly portrayed as crucified’ before the Galatians’ eyes. The verb prographō can mean ‘placarded.’ Bruce argues that the Abraham argument is the backbone of the letter.

hist.context: Paul turns from autobiography to theological argument. His opening question—‘You foolish Galatians! Who has bewitched you?’—combines rebuke with bewildered affection. The argument proceeds by appeal to experience (vv. 1–5) and then to Scripture (vv. 6–9). The experiential argument: the Galatians received the Spirit by hearing with faith, not by doing the law. The scriptural argument reaches to Abraham: he ‘believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness’ (Gen 15:6, v. 6). Those who share Abraham’s faith are Abraham’s true children (v. 7).

sections.header: Verses 10–14 — "Redeemed from the Curse of the Law"

heb.paragraph: The noun katara appears four times in vv. 10–13, creating a theological chain: those who rely on the law are under a curse (v. 10); Christ redeemed us by becoming a curse for us (v. 13). The substitutionary logic is precise: Christ took the curse so that Abraham’s blessing might come to the Gentiles through faith. The OT quotation from Deut 21:23 applies the Deuteronomic curse formula to the crucifixion.

refs.note: The curse formula provides Paul’s premise in v. 10: the law demands comprehensive, continuous obedience.
The prophet’s declaration ‘the righteous will live by faith’ establishes the faith principle as prior to and superior to law-performance. Paul uses the same text in Rom 1:17.
God ‘made him who had no sin to be sin for us’ parallels the substitutionary logic of Gal 3:13.

notes.note: The law’s demand is absolute: not partial obedience, not best-effort, but everything. The law has no grading curve. One failure places the offender under the full covenant curse.

Christ’s substitutionary atonement: he became a curse ‘for us’ (hyper hēmōn). The result is double: the curse is removed from believers, and Abraham’s blessing is extended to the Gentiles. This is penal substitution in its purest form.

Calvin argues that the curse is just: the law’s demands are holy, and the curse falls on those who fail to meet them. Since all have sinned, the entire human race stands under condemnation. Christ’s death is the only remedy: the Son of God became accursed so that the accursed might become sons of God.
The prepositional phrase ex ergōn nomou introduces those who rely on Torah performance. The NET notes that ‘under a curse’ is a state of being.

The verb exagorazō connotes the slave market: Christ purchased believers out of the curse’s domain. First-century Jewish interpreters also applied the hanging curse to crucifixion (cf. 11QTemple 64:6–13).

Moo traces the chain of OT quotations: Deut 27:26 (v. 10), Hab 2:4 (v. 11), Lev 18:5 (v. 12), Deut 21:23 (v. 13). Together they form a complete argument: law demands total obedience; faith is the basis of life; law operates on a different principle from faith; Christ resolved the impasse by absorbing the curse.

Bruce provides detailed analysis of the OT texts, noting that the application of the ‘hanging’ curse to crucifixion was already attested at Qumran (4QpNahum, 11QTemple). The purpose clause of v. 14 is the climax: Christ’s death bridges the Abrahamic promise and its Gentile fulfilment.

hist.context: Paul presents the negative side of the law argument. The logic is syllogistic: (1) the law demands perfect obedience (Deut 27:26, v. 10); (2) no one achieves perfect obedience; (3) therefore all who rely on the law are cursed. The contrast with Hab 2:4 (v. 11) demonstrates that the law operates on a fundamentally different principle from faith. The resolution in v. 13: Christ absorbed the law’s curse through Deut 21:23—crucifixion carries the explicit pronouncement of divine curse.

sections.header: Verses 15–22 — "The Law Does Not Set Aside the Promise"

heb.paragraph: The noun carries a dual sense: legal will or covenant. Paul exploits both in vv. 15–17: just as no one can annul a ratified human will, so the Mosaic law (given 430 years later) cannot annul God’s prior covenant-promise to Abraham. Temporal priority establishes the promise’s inviolability.

The paidagogos (v. 24) was a household slave responsible for supervising a child’s conduct. Paul’s metaphor is deliberately constraining: the law functioned as a temporary custodian until the coming of faith in Christ. Its authority was real but limited—it ended when the child reached maturity.

refs.note: Paul’s statement that the law was brought in ‘so that the trespass might increase’ parallels Gal 3:19: the law’s function was to make sin visible.
Paul’s extended reflection on the law’s function develops the principle stated concisely in Gal 3:19–22.

notes.note: Even a human covenant, once ratified, cannot be altered. God’s covenant with Abraham was ratified by divine oath. The law, given 430 years later, cannot modify its terms. The ‘Seed’ to whom the promises were made is Christ (v. 16).

The law was added temporarily and provisionally. Its purpose was diagnostic, not therapeutic: it identifies the disease of sin but cannot cure it. The law ‘imprisoned everything under sin’ (v. 22) so that the only escape route would be faith in Christ.

Calvin addresses the law’s purpose: by exposing sin and pronouncing condemnation, it drives the sinner to Christ for mercy. Calvin uses the analogy of a mirror: the law shows us our deformity so that we flee to the physician.
The argument from the singular ‘seed’ (sperma) is theological: the Abrahamic promise finds its ultimate fulfilment in a single descendant, Christ.
The phrase tōn parabaseōn charin is ambiguous: the law was added to reveal, provoke, or restrain transgressions. The NET translators favour the revelatory function.

Moo identifies the central hermeneutical principle: temporal priority means theological priority. The law’s role is subordinate and temporary. Moo cautions against reading Paul as anti-law: the law is not opposed to the promises (v. 21) but serves a different function within the same plan.

Bruce provides historical context for the 430-year interval, noting Paul follows the LXX chronology. The mediatorial argument draws on Jewish angelology (cf. Deut 33:2 LXX; Acts 7:53). The paidagogos metaphor is essentially temporal: the custodian’s role ends at the coming of age.

hist.context: Paul addresses the obvious question: if the promise establishes justification by faith, what is the purpose of the law? First, the law was added ‘because of transgressions’ (v. 19)—to make sin visible and condemnable—until the Seed (Christ) should come. Second, the law was given ‘through angels’ and a mediator (Moses), while the promise was given directly by God (v. 20). Paul is careful not to set law and promise in contradiction (v. 21); rather, they serve different functions in God’s single plan.

heb.paragraph: The phrase ‘baptized into Christ’ denotes incorporation into Christ’s person. The accompanying metaphor—‘clothed yourselves with Christ’ (Christon enedysasthe)—signifies a complete change of identity. Together, these images express the participatory dimension of justification.

refs.note: The parallel passage uses the same baptismal imagery: in the new self, there is no Greek or Jew, slave or free, but Christ is all.
Paul’s statement about baptism by one Spirit into one body employs the same pairs and logic as Gal 3:28.

notes.note: The transition from law to faith is a coming of age: the paidagogos’s role is over. This is salvation-historical progression: the law’s era has ended because its purpose has been fulfilled.

Baptism into Christ is an identity transfer. Verse 28 addresses spiritual standing before God: the distinctions are transcended soteriologically. The climax in v. 29: belonging to Christ makes one Abraham’s seed and heir of the promise.

Calvin reads the baptismal imagery as union with Christ: to be ‘clothed with Christ’ means to share in all his benefits. The three pairs of v. 28 remove every barrier to spiritual privilege: before God, the believing slave and believing master stand on identical footing.
The verb enedysasthe is an aorist middle indicative, reflecting a decisive act coinciding with baptism. In the ancient world, the clothing metaphor signified adoption of a new identity.
The NET translators note the shift from ouk ... oude to ouk ... kai in the third pair, echoing the LXX of Gen 1:27, suggesting a new-creational phenomenon.

Moo reads this as the climax of the salvation-historical argument. The declaration of v. 28 eliminates empirical differences as criteria for covenant membership. In Christ, covenant identity is defined solely by faith and baptism. Verse 29 clinches the argument: the very status the Judaizers offered through circumcision is already possessed by those who belong to Christ.

Bruce calls v. 28 ‘the great baptismal reunification formula’ and argues it reflects an early baptismal confession. The three pairs correspond to ethnic, legal, and biological identity categories. The formula may invert the Jewish prayer thanking God for not being a Gentile, slave, or woman.

hist.context: The chapter climaxes with one of the most revolutionary statements in the NT: ‘There is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus’ (v. 28). The three pairs correspond to fundamental social, legal, and biological distinctions. Paul declares that they are no longer determinative of status before God. The phrase ‘male and female’ (arsen kai thēly) echoes Gen 1:27 (LXX), suggesting new creation transcends the old creation’s categories. Verse 29 ties the argument to Abraham: if you belong to Christ, you are Abraham’s seed and heirs.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Appeal to experience: the Spirit came through faith, not law

rows.label: vv.6–9
rows.text: Abraham believed: faith as the principle of righteousness

rows.label: vv.10–12
rows.text: Under the curse: the law demands total obedience

rows.label: vv.13–14
rows.text: Christ redeemed from the curse by becoming a curse for us

rows.label: vv.15–18
rows.text: Promise vs. law: the covenant cannot be annulled

rows.label: vv.19–22
rows.text: Purpose of the law: added because of transgressions, temporary

rows.label: vv.23–25
rows.text: The law as paidagogos: custodian until Christ

rows.label: vv.26–29
rows.text: Sonship through faith; baptized into Christ; all one

lit.note: Galatians 3 is the letter’s theological backbone, moving from experiential appeal through scriptural argument to salvation-historical synthesis. The Abrahamic promise of justification by faith is prior to, superior to, and ultimately fulfilled through Christ, making the Mosaic law a temporary custodian whose role has now ended.

themes.note: Faith and Covenant dominate as Paul demonstrates that the Abrahamic covenant of faith-righteousness is temporally and theologically prior to the Mosaic law. Sovereignty undergirds the argument: God’s unilateral promise cannot be annulled.

hebtext.note: Paul’s argument from experience: the Galatians received the Spirit not through Torah observance but through the hearing of faith (ex akoēs pisteōs, v. 2). The contrast between Spirit and flesh in v. 3 is salvation-historical: ‘flesh’ refers to human effort (especially circumcision), while ‘Spirit’ represents the eschatological gift marking the new covenant era.

In v. 8 Paul asserts that Scripture ‘announced the gospel in advance to Abraham.’ The verb is a hapax legomenon in the NT. By identifying God’s promise to Abraham as a pre-proclamation of the gospel, Paul roots the Gentile mission in the Abrahamic covenant.

The noun katara appears four times in vv. 10–13, creating a theological chain: those who rely on the law are under a curse (v. 10); Christ redeemed us by becoming a curse for us (v. 13). The substitutionary logic is precise: Christ took the curse so that Abraham’s blessing might come to the Gentiles through faith. The OT quotation from Deut 21:23 applies the Deuteronomic curse formula to the crucifixion.

The noun carries a dual sense: legal will or covenant. Paul exploits both in vv. 15–17: just as no one can annul a ratified human will, so the Mosaic law (given 430 years later) cannot annul God’s prior covenant-promise to Abraham. Temporal priority establishes the promise’s inviolability.

The paidagogos (v. 24) was a household slave responsible for supervising a child’s conduct. Paul’s metaphor is deliberately constraining: the law functioned as a temporary custodian until the coming of faith in Christ. Its authority was real but limited—it ended when the child reached maturity.

The phrase ‘baptized into Christ’ denotes incorporation into Christ’s person. The accompanying metaphor—‘clothed yourselves with Christ’ (Christon enedysasthe)—signifies a complete change of identity. Together, these images express the participatory dimension of justification.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: The scriptural argument reaches to Abraham: he ‘believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness’ (Gen 15:6, v…

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 13: Christ absorbed the law’s curse through Deut 21:23—crucifixion carries the explicit pronouncement of divine curse…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Law, the Promise, and Faith

translations.text: I would like to learn just one thing from you: Did you receive the Spirit by the works of the law, or by believing what you heard?
Let me ask you only this: Did you receive the Spirit by works of the law or by hearing with faith?
Are you so foolish? After beginning by means of the Spirit, are you now trying to finish by means of the flesh?
Are you so foolish? Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh?

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: The foundational text for justification by faith. Abraham believed God’s promise, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness—prior to circumcision and prior to the Mosaic law by centuries.

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: God’s promise—‘all peoples on earth will be blessed through you’—is identified in Gal 3:8 as the advance proclamation of the gospel.

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: The curse formula provides Paul’s premise in v. 10: the law demands comprehensive, continuous obedience.

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: The prophet’s declaration ‘the righteous will live by faith’ establishes the faith principle as prior to and superior to law-performance. Paul uses the same text in Rom 1:17.

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: God ‘made him who had no sin to be sin for us’ parallels the substitutionary logic of Gal 3:13.

thread.anchor: The Law, the Promise, and Faith
thread.text: Paul’s statement that the law was brought in ‘so that the trespass might increase’ parallels Gal 3:19: the law’s function was to make sin visible.

discourse.thesis: The Galatians received the Spirit by faith, not law — and Scripture itself proves that justification has always been by faith, that the law cannot give life, and that in Christ all are Abraham's offspring.

nodes.text: The argument from experience: You foolish Galatians! Before whose eyes was Christ publicly portrayed as crucified? Did you receive the Spirit by works of the law or by hearing with faith? Five rhetorical questions force the Galatians to answer from their own spiritual history.

children.text: Having begun by the Spirit, are you now being perfected by the flesh? The contrast Spirit/flesh (πνεῦμα/σάρξ) will structure the rest of the letter.

nodes.text: The argument from Abraham: 'Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness' (Gen 15:6). Therefore those who are of faith (οἱ ἐκ πίστεως) are sons of Abraham. The gospel was announced beforehand to Abraham — in you shall all nations be blessed.

children.text: Those of faith are blessed along with believing Abraham — the Gentile mission is not a novelty but the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise.

nodes.text: The curse of the law: All who rely on works of the law are under a curse (Deut 27:26 — 'everyone who does not abide by all things written'). The law is not of faith — it is based on doing (Lev 18:5). Faith-righteousness and law-righteousness operate on incompatible logics.

Christ became a curse for us (Deut 21:23 — 'cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree') to redeem those under the curse. Purpose: so that the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles in Christ Jesus, so that we might receive the promised Spirit through faith.

The argument from covenant sequence: Even a human covenant, once ratified, cannot be annulled. God's covenant with Abraham was a promise (ἐπαγγελία). The law came 430 years later and cannot annul a prior ratified covenant. If inheritance were by law, it would be no longer by promise — but God gave it to Abraham by promise.

children.text: Paul's exegesis of 'seed' (singular): the promise was not to 'seeds' (many) but to 'your seed' (one) — referring to Christ. This is not careless grammar but a typological reading rooted in the singular form.

nodes.marker: Why then the law? (τί οὖν ὁ νόμος)
nodes.text: Anticipated objection: If the law does not justify, why was it given? Answer: It was added because of transgressions (τῶν παραβάσεων χάριν), until the Seed came. It was ordained through angels by a mediator — implying inferiority to the direct promise to Abraham. The law imprisons under sin so that the promise by faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe.

children.text: Is the law then contrary to the promises? By no means! But the law cannot give life. If it could, righteousness would indeed be by the law.

nodes.marker: Before faith came (Πρὸ τοῦ δὲ ἐλθεῖν τὴν πίστιν)
nodes.text: The law as παιδαγωγός (guardian/tutor): we were held captive under the law, imprisoned until the coming faith would be revealed. The law's custody was temporary and preparatory. Now that faith has come we are no longer under the guardian.

children.text: In Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith. Baptism into Christ means being clothed with Christ. There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female — all are one in Christ Jesus, and if Christ's then Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise. The social leveling is a corollary of the theological argument.

discourse.note: Galatians 3 is Paul's most concentrated biblical argument, drawing on Genesis 15, Deuteronomy 27 and 21, Leviticus 18, and Habakkuk 2 to demonstrate that faith-righteousness antedates the Mosaic law and cannot be displaced by it. The chapter uses three distinct argument types: experiential (vv. 1-5), exegetical (vv. 6-18), and logical (vv. 19-29).
